item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be found under any source as cc-compliant (the closest source being here with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, it is assumed that the image is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and has been removed.

item.title: Re: Site Name
item.content:encoded: There's absolutely zero canon in that regard. While an author or authors may treat a particular site consistently, other works on the site are not expected to follow that.

item.title: Re: Site Name
item.content:encoded:
narrative consistency.

Headcanons widely differ from one another. Sites can be whatever the author needs them to be. Some sites have become consistent through sheer repition, but most haven't.

Narrative consistency is something that various wildly depending on the article, all based on head canon of the author.

item.title: Site Name
item.content:encoded: As much as I hate to be "that guy", isn't it already established that Site-1/Site-01 is just supposed to be a secure data storage, and is not to contain any SCPs on site? It's a small thing, I get it, but there are plenty of numbers to pick from. I would edit it myself, by I decided to check if anyone minds me throwing a few extra digits on. Just trying to maintain a little narrative consistency.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Giving this a -1, for most of the same reasons that have already been discussed. It's barely anomalous, and there are existing agencies that could better contain it. Heck, as the containment procedures themselves mention, the Foundation's demands for secrecy introduce the possibility that modern genetic experimentation might accidentally introduce SCP-1717 into the world. The Foundation's making things worse.

And there could be a story there—that the Foundation sometimes screws up, that their obsession with secrecy sometimes interferes with their goals of safety and security—but that's not really the story presented here. This story is "someone tried to kill humanity by killing all grass, ooh scary."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Footnotes: the SCP!

(seriously though, got enough footnotes there buddy?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 to you, author. This thing not necessarily causing an XK like some sort of uber-omg-it's-totes-powerful SCP, and instead causing a simple cascade effect that could starve out the human race and kill most land based life, tickles my fancy quite nicely.

You don't really realize how terrifying it could be till you think out exactly what would happen if something as small (or big, from this perspective) as the Poaceae family disappeared.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ I think it's because
- of dis

It's italicized because otherwise, on foundation stewarded (that's a word, I swear) land they'd put out the fires they created normally with water or something, while in this case for land where owning the area isn't feasible, they're specifying that they're going to torch it then soak it with XK-35Y.

So if they own the land, they soak it in GH-5Y, burn it, put it out, monitor for 10 years, or till it's safe, and give it up.

If they can't own the land, they soak it in GH-5Y, burn it, then put out the fire with XK-35Y, which, assuming from the XK in the chem title and the lack of post torch monitoring, ensures nothing will ever grow there again.

Extreme, but it gets the job done.

Also, from the way it's written, it's referring to Idiopathic parkinsonISM, which could mean that it would have similar effects to Parkinson's, but isn't necessarily Parkinson's itself, so capitalization isn't really needed.

e/ on second thought, putting that in a collapsible

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
fire shall be suppressed using Foundation herbicide XK-35Y.

Why is the word 'using' italicized in this line? Also, 'parkinson's' should be capitalized.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The fact that this sounds plausible actually makes it more effective IMHO

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this a lot: it's solidly written, if a touch dense (but I liked having to work to understand it), and the effects are truly devastating.

As for the containment question: I agree, it shouldn't be contained by the Foundation. But what you may all be forgetting is that the Foundation isn't perfect. It doesn't always make the best decisions, or the right decisions. It's still run by people. The O5 aren't gods of logic and reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are actually a couple of good candidate compounds for a neuro/hepatotoxin. If I were completely insane about scientific accuracy, I might suggest L-2,3-diaminopropionic acid, which is the hepatotoxic (and mildly neurotoxic) component of the fodder pea toxin complex, and would cause all of the listed symptoms here.

Please do not ask how much time I spent looking for this compound.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but I agree with a lot of the others that it could use a little more. Perhaps just be slightly more overt in hinting that it's man-made.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is about 5% anomalous and 95% "We are all well and truly fucked", but the writing and the sciency-ness is so dense and sexy, I don't care. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Arguments of classification aside, the agent causing the effect should fall within the Foundation's purview.

I mean, technically an alien from outer space isn't really anomalous (after all, it's a perfectly standard organism obeying the laws of physics) and yet it it would be contained. Likewise for "cyborgs", robots, or nanites. They are just technology driven a little over the top, but aren't really "anomalous" in the reality bending sense that people suddenly seem to feel articles should have.

Regardless, well written article.

The thought that a chemical deliberately introduced to the ecosystem by an unnamed source could cause such harm is pretty scary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unless there's already a specialized active-transport mechanism in place to move them, or unless the enzyme can somehow make like a tobamovirus and cram itself through the plasmodesmata*… Yeah, okay, that's stretching it too much. >_>

On the other hand, CCR fragments could very well be small enough for semi-passive uptake. If the author were to add a note that fragments of the protein can induce its expression,** this problem would be solved.

* Which is a really bizarre and fascinating thing. It actually levers the channel open with proteins that basically work like crowbars! More proof that it's hard to come up with a biological SCP freakier and more awesome than what's already alive. :D
** I believe this phenomenon is known in nature, but don't have the time to look it up right at this moment; sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one. It's not especially anomalous, but frankly that's why it works. It sounds plausible, and the damage potential it could cause is mind-boggling. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
enzyme CCR

Molecules of enzymes are too large to passively diffuse through cell walls of the roots of plants, and don't pass through the intestinal lining into the blood stream, so whatever chemical is being produced can't be an enzyme.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, and honestly, the only thing keeping me from upvoting is the uncertainty about whether it's anomalous or merely very unfortunate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do admit to not being a biologist first and foremost (hence my more detailed and angsty rants in computer-related SCPs) so yeah I figured there were things going on that I mistook.

I WAS aware that viral-gene-fuckery (layman's terms FTW) was natural, that was part of the whole "it does all these half-related things that normally shouldn't go together" bit. Which I may have been still wrong about.

I still like the piece, and glad we could at least agree on the keter!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(too widespread and too lethal to really have evolved naturally, for one)

Suppose it's an endogenous retrovirus that codes an enzyme that builds part of a virus protein and confers a survival advantage on the plant by, say, increasing seed production. If nobody had ever created the chemical that activates CCR production, we would never have any idea that it did anything other than those two things. It's hardly unprecedented for a protein to do two things at once, and there are currently a lot of chemical compounds on Earth that have never existed here before, which would never previously have triggered the effect. I mean, if the triggering compound had a perfluorocarbon component, say (to use a really broad example), then there'd never have been an occasion for this to happen.

The fact that the virus is specifically described as an endogenous pararetrovirus also means that there'd be no way to contain it by burning. I mean, you could contain grasses that had already been triggered to produce CCR, but since any other plant in the family could also produce CCR if exposed to the right chemical, and since the Foundation doesn't know what the chemical is, and isn't likely to tell anybody if they find out, we're all boned anyway, whether people burn the prairies or not.

I agree that the SCP is good, and I like it — I swear I do — and I'm not trying to talk you out of upvoting it. I'm just saying that I can't upvote it, because I don't think the knowledge belongs in Foundation custody, and I don't think it's containable in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is literally nothing on Earth that I'm aware of which causes this array of symptoms and then changes its target's genes to pass it along.

Passing along a viral infection genetically through insertion of genes into the host genome: Endogenous retrovirus. Totally natural; not anomalous.

Activation of a latent gene by adding a chemical: gene regulation. See also lac operon, in which gene transcription is triggered by the presence of a specific chemical (in this case lactose). Totally natural; not anomalous.

No, this specific constellation of symptoms doesn't already exist AFAIK. That's a strange standard for what counts as anomalous and what doesn't, though. I mean, as far as I know, nobody's taken a bunch of stickers, stuck them to one another into a ball with a 3-foot diameter, and painted the whole thing fluorescent green, either, but that doesn't mean that if somebody did, it'd be anomalous. None of the components of the effect are unprecedented; only their combination and effect.

This isn't mutation (which is inherently random to some degree) it's controlled biological/genetic manipulation.

Mutation is random. Selection is not random. If the viral infection conferred a survival advantage for the grasses, there's no reason why it couldn't have been perpetuated. (The survival advantage wouldn't have to be related to the effect being described in the piece, either: genes can have more than one effect at a time, and frequently do. All this would have to do would be to slightly increase the number of seeds any particular plant is capable of producing, and you'd have a reproductive advantage. In fact, nobody would ever have known that it could do anything but produce part of a virus and increase seed production, if the chemical that triggers CCR production had never been created.)

it DEFINITELY shouldn't work over a major classification such as "grasses" which covers so many wildly divergent species.

The reason "grasses" exists as a category is because they have similarities to one another and share a more recent common ancestor. The genes of different grass species should be more similar than the genes of a grass and an orchid, as a result. Any similarity in genetic makeup across the species in a family is a possible target for the sort of effect being described here, and if they weren't all similar in genetic makeup, we wouldn't classify them as grasses.

Admittedly this is something of a stretch, but there is, again, no reason to assume that there's anything going on here that is in any way anomalous just because we haven't seen it before. No matter is being created or destroyed, no genes are popping into (or out of) alternate universes, the plants don't need to be sentient, etc. Everything involved in this is still obeying everyday physical laws as far as we know. If the activating compound is some 5-dimensional chemical bullshit, then sure: then we have something anomalous. But the article doesn't say that, and the details the article does include point to non-anomalous material: magnesium and arsenic are both regular-world stuff.

And it's Keter as all hell.

Well, it's certainly extremely dangerous to humanity, certainly. No argument there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These are the reasons I like this thing — these and Echo's. The science is solid, and let me tell you, solidly-written botanical SCPs make me very happy. The Keter reasoning is moderately subtle but also solid: a sudden extinction of Poaceae would mean global ecological collapse, fast enough that even the abrupt extermination of most of the world's staple crops wouldn't necessarily be our biggest problem. The hidden backstory is chill-inducing: why the hell would someone spread this by crop-duster?!

Yeah, it's not as anomalous as a lot of the things we've got contained. Normally, that would bother me. Normally. But this is so well-written, and it's anomalous enough (too widespread and too lethal to really have evolved naturally, for one), that I don't mind.

Also, the containment procedures are a great way to make this hit close to home. It's wildfire season right now where I live, and practically every day, I drive past a new patch of charred earth. Fire is pocking my state with thousands and thousands of scars, many of which will never heal. Every year, as temperatures rise and invasive grasses spread, it gets worse. Now add in the idea that some significant percentage of those burns could exist because the Foundation is trying to save us all from catastrophic extinction… well, it's 100 F out today, and I've got the shivers.

Have my +1, sir. This is good and you should feel good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I see where the anomaly is, and I think I know what could fix it:

SCP-1717-2 (the as-yet-unidentified compound) should be SCP-1717.
SCP-1717 (the condition) should be SCP-1717-1.
SCP-1717-1 (grasses infected by said condition) should be SCP-1717-2.

Why is this anomalous? There is literally nothing on Earth that I'm aware of which causes this array of symptoms and then changes its target's genes to pass it along. This isn't mutation (which is inherently random to some degree) it's controlled biological/genetic manipulation. Via crop duster. By current bio-science logic, that shouldn't work, and it DEFINITELY shouldn't work over a major classification such as "grasses" which covers so many wildly divergent species.

And it's Keter as all hell. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's more the chemical itself that should be the SCP, and the disease would just be the effect.

item.title: Re: Fuck yea
item.content:encoded:
I suspect this is going to have a -EX attached to it a few years after this document was filed

That bothers me, actually. If the Foundation thinks that this will eventually become an -EX, then that means that there's already sufficient evidence that it isn't anomalous, just not fully understood yet. By that reckoning, quantum mechanics is a Keter-level SCP w/ memetic (causes confusion and uncertainty in everyone who studies it) and reality warping capabilities (observation determines outcome, Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle, etc.).

item.title: Re: I dunno
item.content:encoded:
i'm a normal guy studying english, and i understood most of the article (maybe because my parents are agricultural engineers), but this is a little bit heavy and there is no doubt of that.

By the way, i upvoted this with a smile in my face.

item.title: Re: Fuck yea
item.content:encoded:
"We were busy" is a terrible excuse. And the Foundation sitting on nuclear tech for decades actually makes it less explicable, not more: if you could keep it under wraps for decades, why would you stop?

The basic question remains: does the Foundation contain things with no anomalous qualities (i.e. things which are or appear to be explicable with contemporary scientific knowledge), or doesn't it? I'm still saying no, it doesn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Edit: I fail at spelling and not rambling.

Take 2:
There are public angencies that could handle this (such as the EPA) and don't have the added handicap of having to be secret about it. If it is necessary to prevent public knowledge for mass panic reasons (a justification I don't agree with), then hand it to the DHS as a bio-terrorist threat of unknown origin.

Yes, The Foundation should be concerned about this XK level threat. Yes, containing threats is what they do and would be a default response. But once you get past initial response, there is no reason not to let someone else use their man-power, time, and organizational resources to contain it while The Foundation focuses on the problems that can't be handled by someone else.

The CDC can handle plagues, even virulent pandemics. The Foundation takes care of 217.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I can see why the Foundation would secure and contain this even though it isn't conventionally anomalous. For whatever reason the Foundation got to it first. The researcher on the ground saw the potential for this to be really, scarily, world-endingly, bad and moved to get it under control while they studied the mechanism. However the Foundation is inherently a secretive pack-rat which is also involved in a turf-war with several other agencies (most of whom would try to weaponise the heck out of this), so it its instinctive response is to keep it quiet and out of the public gaze. It makes sense from an internal logical POV. After all, who would the Foundation trust with something like this given its civilisation ending potential? Certainly not governments, not the general scientific community (it's almost an item of faith that "The Foundation knows best", and they are deceiving the scientific community as a matter of course on a daily basis), not other agencies (most of those they are ideologically opposed to). No, better to keep it in house with the people who know how to deal with world ending problems. For now at least.

It makes sense to me anyway. I've always seen it as the Foundation not being out to do good or drive science, but being determined to keep situations stable.

item.title: ^ What he said
item.content:encoded: Bio-engineering has come a long way, and even if something like this isn't quite in our real-world grasp yet (at least I hope not), it would be perfectly understandable. Hell, the Foundation already even knows exactly how it does what it does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But people panicking on the revelation of this isn't itself anomalous; that would be a natural, normal response.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: True, it just seemed a little out of the blue.

item.title: Re: Fuck yea
item.content:encoded: Perhaps the Foundation was sitting on nuclear weapons technology for decades. And as for HIV, well, at the time the Foundation probably had its hands full staving off a fanatical parallel universe devoted to our destruction, or something like that. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see this as being anomalous and needing to be contained because of the mass panic it could cause if people were made aware. That alone works for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I wondered this. Especially since the other effects (i.e. ascites and pleural effusion) fit the hepatotoxic nature of the virus.

That said, I saw no reason why the virus couldn't have a secondary neurotoxic effect that causes Parkinsonism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm undecided on this one. On one hand, it's a solidly written scientific entry that will fuck our shit up if not contained. On the other hand, it's a virus that makes zombie plants that are gonna kill your brains.

Like others have said, why do we need to "Secure Contain Protect" this, when humanity would be better served by "Study Contain Publish."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one. The science is solid, the writing is very good, and the concept does scare me. I just wonder why the idiopathic Parkinson's, since there is no direct mention of this affecting dopamine-producing areas? I would think the liver failure would kill them first.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mutation in the virus, not mutation in the plants. (Maybe it's a virus that's been happily restricting itself to the Orchidaceae all this time, and a new mutation allows it to move to the Poaceae, and it happens to be significantly less pleasant in the Poaceae.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can't be a new mutation. Not when it's infecting an entire group of plants whose most recent common ancestor was back in the Cretaceous.

That said, I do agree it needs a clearer anomalous factor. Still upvoting because it's otherwise a terrific article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the whole "destroy all grasses" thing, and think this is a neat way of doing it. The implication that this was deliberate was also nice.

Unfortunately, I've already read this exact thing. I sincerely doubt that you were copycating, especially considering how well detailed this is. But the fact does remain that this is only barely anomalous, if it is at all. I'm going to hold off on voting for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So why wasn't it ever triggered before now?

New mutation in the gene sequence, new chemical in the environment that alters the expression or function of the enzyme coded by the gene, maybe it confers a survival advantage on plants that have it during periods of global warming but not when temperatures are cooler, so it's selected against during some of the planet's history and selected for the rest of the time. There are all sorts of plausible non-anomalous explanations. It doesn't even have to be a man-made trigger.

By their nature, we can never rule out anomalous causes for anything, because anomalous objects can look like anything, be anywhere, and have any effect. But there's nothing in this one that requires it to be anomalous, either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, viruses evolve. They're kind of a borderline case on the living-nonliving spectrum (which really is a spectrum, not a hard delineation). That's what makes HIV so hard to beat, for instance. In contrast, because Smallpox evolved so slowly, we were able to easily wipe it out.
Evolution can really be applied to a lot of things. There's a whole class of computer algorithms built around that premise.
I agree that this thing is odd from an epidemiological perspective, but that's not enough to make it anomalous. It could just be an impressively engineered but purely mundane bioweapon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Same.

I know not all SCPs need to be paranormal or spooky, but this is just a bit too feasible and thus uninteresting to me.

Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An excellent take on Keter. It really works. There are a few spots here and there that could use ironing out (and maybe one or two footnotes to condense into the body, my god), but I will leave that to those with more time than I.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whether or not they're alive (it's not exactly very easy to decide which side of the line they're on) they certainly do evolve.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read a lot of people asking how is this paranormal/anamolous, my thought on the matter is that something this absolutely viruluent is probably not natural, as it would seem something so absolutely dangerous would have either A) already evolved to be less dangerous to its host, so as to not burn out its host or B) already killed everything it could infect and starved the planet, so its sudden, unexplained occurence on the world scene is strange(though ideally that could be better illustrated with a map or notes on a disperal pattern?)

Edit: Wait, do viruses(viri?) evolve? They aren't living…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is scary in its own way, and it's a neat little pointer to something I would concern myself with.
Well done.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice delayed reaction. However, keeping this silent seems extremely detrimental to containment.

Shouldn't the foundation hide the anomalous crop duster, and then under a cover story work with the world to exterminate this? I can't see the Foundation losing anything by exposing this danger. It would be far cheaper to supervise the destruction by outside agencies then suppress knowledge and burn it ourselves.

Because of this I can't bring myself to upvote. However excellently written and clearly a danger that would catch the Foundation's interest. I'll remain neutral for the time being.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See, for me, it's implied anomalous. Yes, the article doesn't come out and say "SCP-1717-2 induces the expression of the SCP-1717-1 gene via [DATA EXPUNGED]", but it's my feeling that it doesn't need to. It seems fairly obviously man-made, but at the same time, the trigger is already waiting in all of the grasses. So why wasn't it ever triggered before now? Either direction you come at it from, it seems fairly obviously anomalous to me, but perhaps I have different ideas of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was hard to understand for a while, but then I re-read it. And Re-read it again. And again. And… wow.

Not bad, I must say. The science (what little of I understand thanks to Wikipedia) is solid. It might be hard for people who don't have degree in botany to understand, though. But the idea's creative and well pulled off, so…

Take my upvote, sir or madam.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So its a genetic disease caused by some chemical. Thats not very anomalous, just dangerous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah. This is one of those "delayed oh SHIT" articles. Consider the following:

  • 2/3 of the human food supply is made up of rice, maize, and wheat (in that order). The planet's carrying capacity is stretched as it is. "Massive starvation" doesn't begin to cover it - this would result in major global depopulation. As in, cut human population in half, easily. All the social disorder which would follow from such a massive die-back would result in further casualties, as would the inevitable wars over the remaining viable food supplies.
  • The majority of domesticated agricultural animals eat grasses, either directly (e.g. grass- or pasture-fed livestock) or indirectly (e.g. livestock fed processed feed, which is often based at least partially from grasses).
  • A great many wild herbivores and omnivores eat (among other things) grasses. Most wild carnivores and omnivores also eat the aforementioned grass-eaters.
  • There is a historical instance of what happens when widespread grasses that hold erosion at bay die. Lets just say it ended badly for all involved.

So, why would the Foundation work its tail off to contain this? Because having it uncontained would risk XK level ramifications, and the Foundation doesn't let that sort of thing happen if it can help it.

Also, I'm a political scientist, not a biologist, and I had little difficulty following. An important thing to note for those who've not had any biology or chemistry: LD50 is short for "median lethal dose", a measurement of the dose of a toxin, radiation, or pathogen required to kill half the members of a tested population after a specified test duration.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm with those who have a concern with 'yeah dangerous but really anomalous?'.

Also I was put off by excessive annotation/footnote stuff. As science-y as it sounds I'm not sure that '24 hour LD50 rat, oral: 2100mg/kg' and '24 hour LD50 D-class, ████: 4800mg/kg' and whatnot are completely necessary and may instead distract the article from the 'real' hook. But, as before,it occurs to me that it's just dangerous, I'm not sure what specifically is the anomalous part. So no vote.

item.title: Re: Fuck yea
item.content:encoded:
They could have contained nuclear weapons before everybody had them, on the grounds that, like with SCP-1717 here, their development could have lead to total global war. Yes, now that nuclear weapons are something we all know about, it'd be dumb to try to contain the technology and knowledge, but there was a point when that wasn't the case. Ditto for HIV.

Can you come up with any examples of things without anomalous properties that the Foundation is currently containing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two thirds of the world's staple foods gone…no corn, wheat, rice, oats, barley. We all switch to potatoes and then the super-blight hits. That is pretty horrific I think.

It probably could be spelled out a bit more though.

item.title: Re: Fuck yea
item.content:encoded: Eh. Containing nuclear weaponry would be a bad idea (Hell, I have read that MAD is the main reason that developed countries don't fight each other so much these days) ,besides, it'd have a bad effect on energetics, and HIV , for all the issues on how it screws over some african countries isn't a world-shattering threat as a condom works as prevention.

item.title: Re: WHAT
item.content:encoded: They speak English in What?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh. That's horrifying in a way, but not much more than a lot of real things.

The hook that actually made me switch vote is that it indeed appears manmade - someone thought that spreading this is a good idea.

item.title: Re: Fuck yea
item.content:encoded:
they take care of anything that might screw humanity over

Even allowing that this is true (it's not my understanding at all), they've really dropped the ball on containing nuclear/chemical/biological weapons and HIV, at minimum; I don't know why they'd make an exception for this.
